items.question: State the MLE objective and the log-likelihood form for i.i.d. data.
items.answer: θ̂_MLE = argmax_θ L(θ) with L(θ) = ∏ᵢ P(xᵢ | θ). Equivalently θ̂_MLE = argmax_θ ℓ(θ) where ℓ(θ) = Σᵢ log P(xᵢ | θ).

items.question: Why is maximizing ℓ(θ) equivalent to maximizing L(θ)?
items.answer: The natural logarithm is strictly increasing on (0, ∞). For any θ₁, θ₂ with L(θ₁) > L(θ₂), we have ℓ(θ₁) > ℓ(θ₂), so both objectives share the same argmax.

items.question: Derive the Bernoulli MLE for parameter p.
items.answer: For k successes in n trials, ℓ(p) = k log p + (n−k) log(1−p). Setting dℓ/dp = k/p − (n−k)/(1−p) = 0 gives p̂ = k/n. Second derivative is negative at interior points, confirming a maximum.

items.question: How does cross-entropy training relate to MLE?
items.answer: For one-hot label y and softmax probabilities π, cross-entropy −Σ y_k log π_k equals negative log-likelihood of the Categorical(π) model. Minimizing average cross-entropy over a dataset is numerical MLE of network parameters.

items.question: When does linear regression MSE equal Gaussian MLE?
items.answer: When targets are modeled as yᵢ = wᵀxᵢ + b + εᵢ with εᵢ ~ N(0, σ²) i.i.d. and σ² treated as fixed, minimizing Σ(yᵢ − ŷᵢ)² maximizes ℓ(w, b) over the mean parameters.
